content.text: Why Telemetry Schemas Need Governance

A metric or span attribute that changes name without warning breaks every dashboard, alert, and SLO that depends on it. In a distributed system where ten services emit the same metric, an uncoordinated rename produces split data — half the time series use the old name, half use the new one — and every aggregation is wrong until both are migrated.

Telemetry schema governance is the set of processes that ensure changes to metric names, label keys, span attributes, and log field names are: proposed before implementation, reviewed for breaking-change impact, deployed with a migration window, and retired only after consumers are migrated.

attrs.alt: Telemetry schema change lifecycle: propose → review → validate → deploy (dual-emit) → retire

content.text: Schema Registry

A schema registry is a version-controlled directory that declares every metric, span attribute, and log field your services emit. It is the source of truth — if a metric is not in the registry, it should not be emitted.
The registry lives in a shared repository (or a dedicated observability repository), not inside individual service repos. Every service that emits telemetry imports from it.

# telemetry-schema/metrics/claude-api.yaml
# Schema version: v2.1.0
# Maintainer: @sre-team

metrics:
  - name: claude_api_requests_total
    type: counter
    description: Total Claude API requests attempted
    labels:
      - key: model
        description: Claude model identifier
        allowed_values: [claude-opus-4-8, claude-sonnet-5, claude-haiku-4-5-20251001]
      - key: status
        description: Request outcome
        allowed_values: [success, timeout, rate_limit, server_error, parse_error, circuit_open]
      - key: service
        description: Service name
        allowed_values: enum  # any value allowed but must be documented
    status: stable   # stable | deprecated | removed
    since: v1.0.0

  - name: claude_api_tokens_input_total
    type: counter
    description: Input tokens sent to Claude API
    labels:
      - key: model
        allowed_values: [claude-opus-4-8, claude-sonnet-5, claude-haiku-4-5-20251001]
    status: stable
    since: v1.0.0

  - name: claude_api_request_latency_ms
    type: histogram
    description: DEPRECATED — use claude_api_request_duration_seconds instead
    status: deprecated
    since: v1.0.0
    deprecated_in: v2.0.0
    removal_in: v3.0.0
    migration: replace with claude_api_request_duration_seconds
Breaking vs Non-Breaking Changes

Not all schema changes require the same migration process. A change is breaking if it invalidates existing queries, dashboards, or alert expressions. Classify each change before deciding the migration path.
Change Type
Breaking?
Migration Required
Rename metric
Yes
Dual-emit period; update all consumers before retiring old name
Add new label to existing metric
Yes (if Prometheus, cardinality increases)
Add label with a default value; announce change; no dual-emit needed if default is correct
Remove label from existing metric
Yes
Dual-emit without the label; update consumers; retire label
Change allowed values for a label
Yes (if narrowing)
Announce before deploying; consumers that filter on the old value will miss data
Add a new metric
No
Just emit it; no consumers depend on it yet
Change histogram buckets
Partial (quantile estimates change)
Dual-emit with old and new bucket config; update recording rules
Add a new span attribute
No
No consumers depend on it yet
Rename span attribute
Yes
Dual-emit; update all TraceQL queries and dashboards

Adding a label to a Prometheus Counter is not as harmless as it looks. The old time series (without the label) and the new ones (with the label) cannot be summed without `without()` clauses. Dashboards break silently until updated.
Schema Linter in CI

A schema linter runs as a CI check on every pull request that touches instrumentation code or the schema registry. It validates three things: new metrics are registered in the registry, label values match the allowed_values list, and no metric has been removed without going through the deprecation lifecycle.

# .github/workflows/telemetry-lint.yaml
name: Telemetry Schema Lint
on: [pull_request]
jobs:
  lint:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: actions/setup-python@v5
        with:
          python-version: "3.12"
      - run: pip install pyyaml
      - run: python3 scripts/lint_telemetry_schema.py
Dual-Emit Migration Pattern

A breaking schema change — a rename, a label restructuring, or a histogram bucket change — requires a dual-emit period. During this period, both the old and new signals are emitted simultaneously. Consumers migrate to the new signal before the old one is retired.

# dual_emit.py — emit both old and new metric during migration window
from prometheus_client import Counter, Histogram

# Old metrics (deprecated in v2.0.0)
claude_request_latency_ms_OLD = Histogram(
    "claude_api_request_latency_ms",
    "DEPRECATED: use claude_api_request_duration_seconds",
    ["model"],
    buckets=[100, 500, 1000, 2000, 5000, 10000, 30000, 60000],
)

# New metrics (stable since v2.0.0)
claude_duration_NEW = Histogram(
    "claude_api_request_duration_seconds",
    "End-to-end Claude API request duration",
    ["model"],
    buckets=[0.1, 0.5, 1.0, 2.0, 5.0, 10.0, 30.0, 60.0],
)


def observe_latency(model: str, elapsed_seconds: float) -> None:
    """Emit both metrics during the migration window."""
    # New metric (seconds)
    claude_duration_NEW.labels(model=model).observe(elapsed_seconds)
    # Old metric (milliseconds) — remove after v3.0.0 deployment
    claude_request_latency_ms_OLD.labels(model=model).observe(elapsed_seconds * 1000)

Track which dashboards and alerts still query the old metric. A Prometheus query `count(group_left() claude_api_request_latency_ms)` tells you how many time series are actively being scraped. Once the count reaches zero and you have confirmed that no alerts reference the old name, the dual-emit code can be removed.
Versioning the Schema Registry
The schema registry is versioned using semantic versioning: MAJOR.MINOR.PATCH.
Version Bump
Trigger
Example
MAJOR
Removing a metric or span attribute; changing label cardinality structure
v2.0.0 → v3.0.0 when claude_api_request_latency_ms is fully retired
MINOR
Adding a new metric, span attribute, or approved label value
v2.0.0 → v2.1.0 when claude_circuit_breaker_state is added
PATCH
Documentation updates, description corrections, no schema change
v2.1.0 → v2.1.1 when a description typo is fixed

Tag each release in version control. Services declare which schema version they implement in their deployment configuration. This makes it possible to audit which services are behind and need to migrate.

# service deployment config excerpt
metadata:
  name: claude-worker
  annotations:
    telemetry.schema/version: "v2.1.0"
    telemetry.schema/repo: "github.com/yourorg/telemetry-schema"
    telemetry.schema/status: "current"  # current | migration-in-progress | stale
Deprecation and Retirement Process
Retiring a metric follows a four-step process that protects all consumers from silent data loss:
Mark the metric as deprecated in the registry. Set deprecated_in to the current version and removal_in to the next major version. This serves as the official notice to consumers.
Start the dual-emit period. The service emits both the old and new metric. Duration: at minimum 2 full weeks and until all known consumers have migrated.
Announce in the team channel (or engineering-wide Slack if cross-team). Include the removal date, the new metric name, and a migration guide (a one-line PromQL rename is usually sufficient).
Remove the old metric instrumentation. Update the registry entry to status: removed. The metric disappears from Prometheus scrapes immediately — consumers that did not migrate will see gaps.

The minimum dual-emit window is 2 weeks, not 2 days. Dashboards and alerts often reference metrics in ways that are only noticed during an incident. A 2-week window ensures at least one on-call rotation cycle sees the new signal and can verify their dashboards.
Schema Change Review Checklist
Use this checklist when reviewing a PR that proposes a schema change. The reviewer signs off only when all items are checked.
Check
Reviewer Action
Is the change in the schema registry YAML?
If not: request the proposer adds it before merging
Is the change classified correctly (breaking vs non-breaking)?
Review the table in the Breaking vs Non-Breaking section; override classification if wrong
For breaking changes: is a dual-emit period planned?
Reject the PR if dual-emit code is absent for a rename or removal
Are all known consumers listed?
Search for the old metric name in the dashboards repo, alerts repo, and runbooks; add missed consumers to the PR description
Has a retirement date been proposed?
For deprecations, verify removal_in is set to the correct next major version
Does the CI linter pass?
Check the GitHub Actions run; do not override a failing linter without SRE lead approval
Automating Consumer Discovery
Manually searching for consumers of a metric is error-prone. Automate it by scanning all dashboard JSON and alert YAML files for references to the old metric name.

# Run before proposing retirement of a metric
python3 scripts/find_metric_consumers.py claude_api_request_latency_ms
# Consumers of claude_api_request_latency_ms:
#   dashboards/claude-latency.json: lines [14, 52, 88]
#   alerts/claude-slo-rules.yaml: lines [23]
# → 2 files must be migrated before retirement is safe
Governance for Log Fields
Log fields (structured JSON keys) require the same governance as metric labels. An engineer who renames level to severity breaks every log query that filters by log level across every service.
Field
Type
Description
Status
timestamp
ISO 8601 string
Event timestamp in UTC
stable
level
string
Log severity: debug, info, warn, error, fatal
stable
service
string
Service name — must match metric service label
stable
trace_id
string
OTel trace ID for correlation with spans
stable
span_id
string
OTel span ID for correlation with spans
stable
model
string
Claude model name (same allowed_values as metric label)
stable
error_type
string
Error class (same allowed_values as metric error_type label)
stable
request_id
string
Anthropic X-Request-Id header value (OK in logs, forbidden in metrics)
stable
Summary

Telemetry schema governance prevents breaking changes from silently corrupting dashboards, alerts, and SLO calculations. It is an operational process, not a technical constraint — the key tools are a schema registry, a CI linter, and a dual-emit migration pattern.
Maintain a schema registry in version control with one YAML file per telemetry domain (Claude API, worker, etc.). Every emitted metric must be registered.
Classify changes as breaking or non-breaking before implementation. Breaking changes require a dual-emit period and consumer migration before retirement.
Run a schema linter in CI that checks all emitted metric names against the registry and rejects unregistered or removed metrics.
Use dual-emit during breaking migrations: emit both old and new signals for at least 2 weeks while consumers migrate.
Version the registry with semver: MAJOR for removals, MINOR for additions, PATCH for documentation.
Follow the same deprecation lifecycle for log fields as for metric names — any structured key change is a breaking change for log queries.
